It's kind of like the political thing of saying the person most likely to get elected president is the person you'd like to have a guys have a beer with, the women would trust babysitting their kid. And there's been very few deviations from that, you know, over the years. And so they're trying to find, if you will, call it authenticity, call it humanity, whatever. That's what they're really looking for. And it's far more important than the information. So like your book editors, if you loaded a book up with, you know, the 212 steps of a case presentation and, you know, that kind of stuff, which obviously you could, it doesn't work. It doesn't do it. Anybody else? I think you said, well, how would you use that book on case presentation? How? Tell us what the template of that book would be. So to use that example, in a sense, you'd be an idiot to do a cheap thing on case presentation. But in his environment, and where he's talking about 40 and 50 and $60,000 cases. So let's say the guy takes that down in his head and is thinking, well, if he can get 60, maybe I can get 20, right? You're still talking about, say, 20, 25,000 bucks. So now you're back to our transaction value argument of just one, you wouldn't have got otherwise. Boom, right? So that price point should be in the multiple, 10,000, 15,000. If I was going to do a three-day boot camp on it, it would be a $15,000 seminar. So now there's a huge argument not to put it in a book or a 4CD program, you know, or whatever. At least until you went and milked it from the other direction. So now if you weren't going to do it at all, you're right. So I see it would be a book that essentially told stores. So its job would be to convince people that they could get big cases, which there's, in his case, there's people who don't think they're clinically capable of doing that. You'd have to think of all the reasons, so they don't think they can sell to do that. They don't think their market will support those kind of prices. So your stories would be about convincing them that they could do it, right? Motivating them to want to do it, why it's better to have that kind of practice than a, than an in the front door, drill them, shove them out the back door kind of practice. And a whole lot of it wouldn't be about case presentation at all, even though you said it was a book about case presentation. Because it would be all these other kinds of stories somehow barely stretched to connect to that topic, but to do the things on our 31 list, thereby setting up the sale of seminar coaching whatever. You gave it to us a while back. We used it for tele-seminars. What needs to be done and what not to do, but not how to do anything. Yeah. See, the classic mistake authors make, speakers make, is they think, and I did it early when I spoke, that if you just teach the hell out of it, right, and you give them just mountains of information, they are going to see so much value now that they're going to want everything you got, and they're going to, you know, so like, if we did that in one book, that should ensure everybody going back to the bookstores and wanting all the other Kennedy books because there was so much teaching in there. Doesn't work, right? So you're right. I mean, the format, the format is always to tell them what to do, but not really tell them how to do it. So like people, like for example, if you're going to pepper anything in with the stories, and if you look at, again, thinking of Rich as a model, you do checklists, you do the seven of this and the nine of this and the 14 of that, and now they think they got something, right? But what do you do with it now that you got the checklist, right? So if I covered all this in a couple of chapters and gave you the 31 list, somebody think they got something, but ultimately when they go to work with it, what the hell are they going to do with the third, you know, there's not enough understanding, there's not enough reference for it, or you give them like brain dead easy little tools. So again, they feel like you gave them something and they got something they can fill out some lines on, but it really doesn't lead anywhere. Well, if you're really, see, that's actually a reference book. So that's not an advocacy book. Okay, but why did you do it when you make a lot of money doing copywriting? You have much higher end courses on copywriting? It doesn't get in the way of that the way that book's done, if anything, so you could think about that in terms of this case presentation book. See, it doesn't get in the way of that because it only gives them pieces. So like you could come away from that and you could write some decent headlines and you kind of got a structure. And for the true small business buyer who would never be a client of mine anyway, they could only be a customer, right? Where there's, they're the only one delivering anything halfway decent to their prospect. People can work out of that book by itself and actually get somewhere. But the type of person who would hire me, all that's going to do is bring them the next step closer to me because what they can do out of that is not good enough, but it demonstrates competence. You know, it shows them a model that's different than what they've seen, but it is a reference book more than it is an advocacy book. And knowing what I know now, I probably wouldn't have written it, although it has, you know, it's earned its keep and it stayed on the shelf a long time because people do use it like words that sell and, you know, they keep it to look at the templates and look at the structures and look at the headline lists. It's sort of like how to write a good advertisement from Victor Schwab, which I keep around because the headline list is in it. But other than that, there's really not much you can do as a result of having that book. You sort of have a general idea that these ads are bad and these ads are good, but exactly how to make, you don't have it. But the nice thing about reference books is they stay around forever. You know, if they get some traction, they're there forever, but they're not, they don't really create any kind of emotional bonding and emotional movement. Those books over the years have probably brought, they brought speaking engagements and consulting assignments and brought a lot of customers. No BS books do the opposite. I hardly ever get a speaking inquiry because of them, but we get customers. So you feel like your demonstration of confidence gets you the speaking engagements? Yeah, it's like come and teach this to our guys. So would you say, I mean, I'd like to hear a little bit more about what you, I understand the advocacy books, the reference books. I mean, like I've seen, you know, I think a good model, I mean, John Kramer, a thousand ways to market your books, there's so much content in there that you like have to have him consult with you to sort it all out. You know, in a certain way. Hmm? So much and you see, see, know so much, you know, I mean, that's probably a decent reference. Yeah, but now here, so if we take Kramer as an example, his consulting practice has always stayed cheap and always stayed small, right? Because that's the only reason they're coming to him. Right. There's no big emotional connection. There's no, I want to hang around John. I want to know more about what John's about. See, there isn't any of that. Now, if you look at the most recent additions of sales letter and marketing plan and you compare them to the previous additions, I've sort of fixed that a little as best I could within the framework that I've been allowed to work in. I mean, you got to remember sales letter. First of all, sales letter was written in 1991 and it was written in a very simple way. The agent called and said, Adams wants you to do a book on something. What can you do a book on? And I had a manual called how to write a million dollar sales letter, which we were selling for 199 bucks. I said, well, that'll, that'll be a book and I can get it done in a weekend. You know, so there was not a lot of strategic thought to this. But now if you compare the current addition to the first addition, it's fatter, but the fat isn't more teaching. The fat is getting in some stories and getting in some, some personal stuff. And the same with marketing plan, which was done in 93. So, you know, that's a long time ago. And I mean, 10 years from now, you'd look at the No BS books and they'll be 82 things. I'll think should we cut it on that better. You're right. Speaking of you do the pretty much an advocacy book. It just did you have more plugs about how you were speaking here? Yeah. I pile up stuff. So like the affluent book is getting done now, right? So I started maybe six months ago.
